event: Early Homo sapiens fossils are deposited at Jebel Irhoud, later recognized as among the oldest known anatomically modern humans.
Iberomaurusian hunter-gatherer cultures flourish across the Maghreb, leaving sites like Taforalt with early human burials.
Neolithic communities adopt farming and herding, and Berber (Amazigh) populations establish themselves across the region.
Phoenician traders found coastal settlements such as Lixus and trading posts along the Atlantic and Mediterranean shores.
Carthaginian influence dominates Morocco's coastal trade, and the explorer Hanno reportedly sails along the Atlantic coast.
The Berber kingdom of Mauretania emerges as an organized state under local kings allied with Rome.
Juba II becomes client king of Mauretania and develops Volubilis and Iol (Caesarea) as cultural centers.
Emperor Caligula has King Ptolemy of Mauretania killed, triggering revolt and Roman annexation.
Rome organizes the territory as the province of Mauretania Tingitana, centered on Tingis (Tangier) and Volubilis.
Rome withdraws from much of Mauretania Tingitana, retaining only northern coastal enclaves as Berber autonomy reasserts.
Vandals crossing into North Africa weaken Roman control, and Berber polities reassert independence in the interior.
Arab general Uqba ibn Nafi campaigns westward across the Maghreb, reaching the Atlantic and beginning the Islamic conquest.
Berber and Arab Muslim forces under Tariq ibn Ziyad cross from Morocco into Iberia, launching the conquest of al-Andalus.
The Great Berber Revolt against Umayyad rule spreads across Morocco, asserting local Kharijite-influenced autonomy.
Idris I founds the Idrisid dynasty, establishing the first major Muslim state and Arab-Islamic rule in Morocco.
Idris II makes Fez the dynasty's capital, and it grows into a leading center of learning and Islamic scholarship.
The Qarawiyyin mosque and university is founded in Fez, often cited among the world's oldest continually operating universities.
The Almoravids found Marrakesh as their capital and build a vast empire spanning Morocco, the western Sahara, and al-Andalus.
The Almohads capture Marrakesh and replace the Almoravids, forging an empire across North Africa and Muslim Spain.
Almohad construction projects flourish, including the Koutoubia Mosque, reflecting the empire's cultural zenith.
Almohad forces are crushed at Las Navas de Tolosa in Iberia, accelerating the dynasty's decline.
The Marinid dynasty takes Marrakesh, ending Almohad rule and governing Morocco from Fez.
Portugal captures Ceuta, beginning European seizure of Moroccan Atlantic and Mediterranean ports.
The Saadian dynasty seizes power, unifying Morocco and resisting Ottoman and Iberian expansion.
Moroccan forces destroy a Portuguese invasion at the Battle of the Three Kings (Alcacer Quibir), securing independence.
Sultan Ahmad al-Mansur's army crosses the Sahara and conquers the Songhai Empire, capturing Timbuktu and its gold trade.
Moulay al-Rashid founds the Alaouite dynasty, which still reigns in Morocco today.
Sultan Moulay Ismail begins a long reign, making Meknes a grand imperial capital and centralizing the state.
Morocco becomes one of the first nations to recognize the independent United States, opening early diplomatic ties.
Morocco and the United States sign a treaty of friendship, among the longest unbroken U.S. treaty relationships.
France defeats Morocco at the Battle of Isly amid the conflict over French expansion in neighboring Algeria.
Spain wins the Hispano-Moroccan War, gaining territory and indemnities that deepen European pressure on Morocco.
The Algeciras Conference internationalizes oversight of Morocco amid rivalry between European powers.
The Treaty of Fez establishes a French protectorate over most of Morocco, with Spain controlling northern and southern zones.
Rif Berber forces under Abd el-Krim crush Spain at Annual and proclaim the short-lived Rif Republic.
Combined French and Spanish forces defeat Abd el-Krim, ending the Rif War and consolidating colonial control.
The Istiqlal Party issues a manifesto demanding full Moroccan independence from France.
France exiles Sultan Mohammed V, sparking widespread nationalist resistance and unrest.
Mounting pressure forces France to allow Mohammed V to return from exile to Morocco.
Morocco gains independence from France and Spain, and joins the United Nations the same year.
King Hassan II ascends the throne following the death of Mohammed V.
Morocco fights Algeria in the brief Sand War over disputed Saharan border territory.
Hassan II launches the Green March, sending civilians into Western Sahara as Spain withdraws.
Morocco annexes much of Western Sahara, triggering a long armed conflict with the Polisario Front.
A UN-brokered ceasefire halts the Western Sahara war, with a promised referendum left unresolved.
King Mohammed VI succeeds Hassan II and begins cautious political and social reforms.
Suicide bombings in Casablanca kill dozens, prompting a major crackdown on extremism.
A reformed family law code, the Moudawana, significantly expands women's legal rights.
Amid Arab Spring protests, a referendum approves a new constitution that limits some royal powers.
Morocco rejoins the African Union after a 33-year absence over the Western Sahara dispute.
Morocco normalizes relations with Israel as the U.S. recognizes its sovereignty over Western Sahara.
A powerful earthquake in the High Atlas near Marrakesh kills thousands and devastates mountain villages.
